zones.label: L293D
facts: Motor channels: 2 independent DC channels (or 1 stepper)
Motor voltage: 4.5 V–36 V (Vmotor pin)
Logic voltage: 5 V (TTL; 3.3 V GPIO usually works)
Continuous current: 600 mA per channel
Peak current: 1.2 A per channel
Protection: thermal shutdown; internal flyback diodes
Package: DIP-16 — breadboard friendly, no soldering
Typical cost: $0.50–$1.50
Best for: beginners, breadboard prototyping, lower-current motors

zones.label: DRV8833
zones.summary: Low-voltage H-bridge — direct 3.3 V logic connect
facts: Motor channels: 2 independent DC channels (or 1 stepper)
Motor voltage: 2.7 V–10.8 V
Logic voltage: 2.7 V–5 V (3.3 V GPIO direct connect)
Continuous current: 1.5 A per channel
Peak current: 2 A per channel
Protection: current limiting, thermal shutdown, nSLEEP low-power mode
Package: HTSSOP-16 (surface-mount; usually on a breakout board)
Typical cost: $1.00–$3.00 (breakout board)
Best for: Pico robots, 3.3 V logic, slightly more current than L293D

zones.label: TB6612FNG
zones.summary: Efficient dual H-bridge — high torque, clean PWM
facts: Motor channels: 2 independent DC channels + separate PWM input each
Motor voltage: 2.5 V–13.5 V
Logic voltage: 2.7 V–5.5 V (3.3 V GPIO direct connect)
Continuous current: 1.2 A per channel
Peak current: 3.2 A per channel (burst, up to 2 s)
Protection: thermal shutdown, under-voltage lockout, standby (STBY pin)
Package: SSOP-24 (surface-mount; usually on a breakout board)
Typical cost: $2.00–$5.00 (breakout board)
Best for: higher-torque robots, long battery sessions, clean PWM speed control

quiz.question: Which driver runs on the highest motor voltage, up to 36 V?
quiz.explanation: The L293D accepts a motor supply of 4.5 V–36 V on its Vmotor pin — the widest high-voltage range of the three. The DRV8833 tops out at 10.8 V and the TB6612FNG at 13.5 V.

quiz.question: Which driver gives the highest peak current per channel (3.2 A in a short burst)?
quiz.explanation: The TB6612FNG can push 3.2 A per channel for a burst of up to 2 seconds, making it the best pick for higher-torque robots. The L293D peaks at 1.2 A and the DRV8833 at 2 A.

quiz.question: Which driver is a DIP-16 chip you can plug straight into a breadboard with no soldering?
quiz.explanation: The L293D ships in a breadboard-friendly DIP-16 package, so beginners can prototype without soldering. The DRV8833 (HTSSOP-16) and TB6612FNG (SSOP-24) are surface-mount and come on breakout boards.

quiz.question: Which driver has an nSLEEP low-power mode and built-in current limiting?
quiz.explanation: The DRV8833 adds current limiting and an nSLEEP low-power mode on top of thermal shutdown — handy for battery-powered 3.3 V systems. The L293D and TB6612FNG do not list an nSLEEP pin.

quiz.question: Which driver uses a single STBY pin to put both motor channels into standby?
quiz.explanation: The TB6612FNG has one STBY (standby) pin that controls both channels, plus 2 direction pins and 1 PWM pin per channel. That standby control helps save power between moves.
